message.content:
You are confirmer #2, independent. Confirm this fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (the named test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Read the actual changed file + test at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
Fix J-002 in scripts/capture_transcripts.py
What: operator full display name redacted (from git user.name / ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES), whitespace-flexible + length-guarded, applied in _scrub.
Test(s): test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name
Verdict CONFIRMED or CONCERN; be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    try:
90	        import subprocess
91	
92	        out = subprocess.run(
93	            ["git", "config", "user.name"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5
94	        )
95	        if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
96	            names.append(out.stdout.strip())
97	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
98	        pass
99	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
100	    for n in names:
101	        if len(n) < 4:
102	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words
103	        # match the name with flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]")
104	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split()) + r"\b"))
105	    return pats
106	
107	
108	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
109	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
110	
111	
112	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
113	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
114	        if pat.groups == 2:
115	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
116	        elif pat.groups == 1:
117	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
118	        else:
119	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
120	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
121	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
122	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
123	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
124	    # Redact the operator's full display name too (#J-002) — distinct PII the username/email rules
125	    # leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
126	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
127	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
128	    return text
129	
130	
131	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
132	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
133	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
134	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
135

content.text: The test loads the module fresh via `_load`. The patterns are computed at module level (`_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()`), so the env var must be set before the module loads. Let me verify `_load` re-imports and check it discriminates.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -n "_load\b" tests/test_scripts.py | head -3 && echo "---DEF---" && sed -n '1,40p' tests/test_scripts.py

content.content:
17:def _load(rel: str, name: str):
28:    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts")
45:    gate = _load("scripts/license_gate.py", "license_gate_fd")
---DEF---
"""Guards for repo scripts + examples (pass 3: #F-051, #F-048).

These exercise non-package files (scripts/, examples/) that the wheel never ships but that run
on every commit (transcript capture) or are copied by users (the example). Where a test targets
a file's *content* it reads THAT file and asserts both the corrected form and the absence of the
stale/insecure form (the artifact-vs-test fidelity rule).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import importlib.util
from pathlib import Path

_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent


def _load(rel: str, name: str):
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(name, _ROOT / rel)
    assert spec and spec.loader
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    return mod


def test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars():
    # #F-051: a bearer token containing base64 chars (+ / =) must be FULLY redacted, not split at
    # the first such char leaving a trailing fragment in the transcript.
    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts")
    text = "Authorization: [REDACTED]"
    scrubbed = cap._scrub(text)
    assert "[REDACTED]" in scrubbed
    # no recognizable token fragment survives
    for frag in ("eyJhbGc", "payload/signature", "/signature", "iOiJIUzI1NiJ9"):
        assert frag not in scrubbed, f"token fragment {frag!r} leaked past redaction"


def test_license_gate_closes_the_report_file(tmp_path):
    # #F-050: the report must be opened via a context manager so the fd is closed deterministically.
    # Discriminating: intercept the module's open() and capture the file handle; after main()
    # returns, assert it is .closed. A bare json.load(open(...)) leaves the handle open (closed

content.text: `_load` does `exec_module` fresh each call, so module-level `_DISPLAY_NAME_RES` is recomputed with the monkeypatched env set beforehand. Good — the test will pick up "Ada Lovelace". Now let me run the test and a revert check.
Now the discrimination check — revert the J-002 logic and confirm the test fails.

content.text:
That test passes on a clean tree — it was failing only transiently during my revert experiment (the backup restore overlapped). All clean now. The failure was an artifact of my own test manipulation, not the fix.

Let me do final review of the implementation quality: the length guard (`< 4`), whitespace-flexible join, env + git sourcing, and the `\b` boundaries.

input.description: Final full scripts test run on clean tree

content.text: All 11 pass on clean tree. Verification complete.

input.reasoning:
Read the changed file scripts/capture_transcripts.py and the test tests/test_scripts.py::test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

APPROPRIATE: The fix matches the description exactly. _display_name_patterns() (lines 76-105) sources candidate names from both ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES (comma-split, stripped) and `git config user.name` (subprocess, returncode-checked, wrapped in a broad except so git absence never breaks capture). It is length-guarded (`if len(n) < 4: continue`) to avoid over-redacting short common words, and builds whitespace-flexible regexes via `re.compile(r"\b" + r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split()) + r"\b")` so "Ada   Lovelace" with multiple spaces is also matched, with \b word boundaries and re.escape preventing regex injection. The patterns are precomputed at module load (_DISPLAY_NAME_RES) and applied in _scrub (lines 124-127) after the username/email/secret rules, which is the correct order (so it catches names left in cleartext beside already-redacted emails). Idiomatic and no regression: full tests/test_scripts.py (11 tests incl. bearer-token, invalid-utf8, license-gate) all pass on a clean tree.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: With the fix in place the named test passes. I reverted only the J-002 application loop in _scrub and re-ran — the test FAILED with "'Ada Lovelace' is contained here: Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>..." confirming it specifically asserts the display name is gone (and separately that the email is still scrubbed and the flexible-whitespace form is caught). The test correctly relies on _load() doing a fresh exec_module after monkeypatch.setenv, so module-level pattern computation picks up ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES.

Note: a transient failure of test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 I observed was an artifact of my own backup/restore overlap during the revert experiment; on the verified-clean working tree (git status empty) all 11 tests pass.

content.text: CONFIRMED. Fix J-002 in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py is both appropriate and complete. The test discriminates (fails when the redaction loop is reverted), the implementation is length-guarded and whitespace-flexible with safe git/env sourcing, and the full scripts test suite passes on a clean tree.
